title: A practicable method for the analysis of complex motion of biological and soft matter
abstract: Biological function of living matter is fulfilled by complex motions of biological and soft matter. Unlike general motion is deterministic described by Newton's laws, these motions are mostly random and uncertain for the position in stochastic process, being characterized as irregular trajectories of movement without a defined velocity. Like human fingerprint, the trajectory is the identity of the motion containing fundamental dynamical information. Such irregular trajectories randomly inter-wind and twist to each other to produce a complicated turmoil configuration in which so far the unrealized mechanism of motion is hidden. Nowadays, the analytical method for this fingerprint trajectory is still missed. Here we develop a practicable method to decipher complicated trajectory configuration, which uncovers abundant dynamical information hiding in irregular trajectories, revealing the remarkable evolution of spatial-temporal micro-structure, thus leading to the novel systematic study of the dynamics of biological and soft matter.
summary: This paper presents a new analytical method for studying the complex, random motions found in biological and soft matter. By treating the stochastic trajectories of particles like Brownian motion as propagating wave packets, the author can extract hidden dynamical information from seemingly random paths. The method bridges the gap between microscopic ballistic steps and macroscopic diffusion. This approach allows for a systematic study of how spatio-temporal microstructures evolve in complex fluids.
main_problem: The lack of an analytical method to decipher the complex, stochastic 'fingerprint' trajectories of biological and soft matter to extract underlying dynamical information.
main_result: The development of a method that maps the Gaussian probability distribution of Brownian particle displacements onto a propagating wave packet, allowing for the extraction of microscopic dynamical information from irregular trajectories.
method: A quantization-inspired approach that transforms the probability density of particle displacement into a wave function, using Fourier transforms and Taylor expansions of the angular frequency to relate ballistic motion to wave packet dynamics.
model_or_system: A 1D model of a spherical Brownian particle (e.g., proteins, bacteria, or silica beads) undergoing a series of random ballistic motion steps in a fluid, driven by a random thermal field.
key_observables: Mean-squared displacement, probability distribution P(x,t), group velocity (vg), mean displacement, and diffusion constant (D).
important_parameters: Ballistic step length (l), step time (tb), diffusion exponent (alpha), diffusion constant (D), and effective mass (m*).
main_assumptions: The initial distribution of the wave function is Gaussian, the number of extra steps follows a Gaussian distribution, and the time intervals are integer multiples of the ballistic step time.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows irregular Brownian trajectories and a 1D model of a silica bead performing ballistic motion with direction flipping. Figure 2 illustrates the wave packet propagation mechanism, showing how oscillating motion and drift lead to net displacement.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of analyzing irregular trajectories, proposes a wave packet quantization model, derives the mathematical framework for the wave function, maps microscopic parameters to macroscopic observables, and concludes with the implications for studying soft matter dynamics.
why_interesting: The paper uses a mathematical analogy between Brownian motion uncertainty and quantum mechanical wave packets, providing a framework for treating stochastic classical trajectories using tools similar to those used in open quantum systems and wave mechanics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Irregular trajectories of Brownian motion, inset: a segment of trajectory ‘ab’ consisted of a number of steps produced by the ballistic motion of Brownian particle. (b) One-dimensional trajectories of a 3 µm diameter silica bead in air at 2.75 kPa performs ballistic motion around the equilibrium position ‘0’ due to consistently flipping the motion direction [10]. (c) The ensemble effect of random ballistic motion is equivalent to a vibration. An arrow represents one step of ballistic motion. (d) The segment of trajectory (e.g.,‘ab’) is the displacement (x) of particle observed under optical microscopy, whose probability density P displays a Gaussian distribution. (e) The...

Figure 2: (a) The wave packet moving along x axis is expected to reach the position A′, but it actually arrives at the position A due to the oscillating motion. (b) The propagation mechanism of wave packet: the oscillating motion plus drift motion. A oscillating motion consists of the forward motion ①and the backward motion ②, so as to the motion ③④. After the oscillation motion ①②③④⑤, the wave packet drifts to ⑥ due to a fluctuation of random field along the positive x direction, producing a drift motion with a displacement d. The wave packet is continually repeating above process until producing the net displacement L, which corresponds to the displacement in (a) with OA = L

Figure 3: The fluctuation of wave packet (particle) position due to the asymmetry of the forward and backward motion of wave packet. (a) Symmetrical distribution of the forward and backward motion of wave packet, represented by the pace numbers. ‘0’ pace is the mean position of wave packet (particle) with no fluctuation. The probability of wave packet (particle) distribution decreases with the pace number increasing. (b) The position fluctuation of wave packet (particle) enlarges with temperature increasing. ‘0’ corresponding to ‘0’ pace in (a).
